title: Pollo alla diavola
subtitle: Spatchcocked grilled chicken with chilli, lemon, and garlic
excerpt: Devil's chicken: a whole bird spatchcocked flat, marinated in chilli, lemon, garlic, and olive oil, then grilled or roasted hot and fast until the skin crackles and the meat stays juicy. The flat shape means it cooks evenly in half the time of a roast chicken.
sourceNotes: Pollo alla diavola is a central and southern Italian preparation, documented from the nineteenth century. The name refers to the spicy chilli heat, likened to hellfire. Artusi includes a grilled chicken alla diavola in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain), describing a bird pressed flat and cooked over coals. The preparation is particularly associated with Rome and Lazio, where it appears on the menus of traditional trattorias.

content.text: is that a flat bird cooks faster and more evenly than a round one. The breast and legs finish at the same time. More skin is exposed to direct heat, so more of it crisps. A 1.4 kg chicken that would take 1 hour 20 minutes in a conventional roast takes 40 minutes flat, with better skin.

The marinade is simple: chilli, lemon, garlic, rosemary, and olive oil. Rub it under the skin where possible as well as over it. The chilli should be present but not overwhelming, this is supposed to taste of chicken with heat, not just heat. A minimum of 2 hours marinating; overnight is better.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: 1 zested and juiced for marinade, 1 halved for serving

items.name: Chilli flakes
items.prepNote: or 1 fresh red chilli, finely chopped

content.text: Place the {{whole-chicken}} breast-side down on a board. Using heavy scissors or a sharp heavy knife, cut along both sides of the backbone from tail to neck. Remove and discard the backbone (or save for stock). Turn the bird breast-side up, open it out flat, and press down firmly on the breastbone with both hands until you hear and feel it crack flat. The bird should now lie completely flat.

Mix the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil with the {{garlic}} of garlic, the {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, the chopped {{rosemary}} rosemary leaves, the zest and juice of one {{lemon}}, and a generous amount of salt and pepper. Rub the mixture all over the chicken and push some under the skin of the breast and legs. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight.

Remove the chicken from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Preheat the oven to 220°C (fan 200°C). Place the chicken skin-side up in a roasting tin or directly on the oven rack with a tray underneath to catch drips. Roast for 35 to 40 minutes until the skin is deeply golden and crackled and the juices run clear when the thickest part of the thigh is pierced. If finishing on a barbecue, grill over medium-high indirect heat for 30 minutes, then move to direct heat for 5 to 8 minutes to crisp the skin.
Rest the bird for 10 minutes before cutting. Serve with the halved lemon, squeezed over at the table, and any pan juices. Good with bitter leaves, roasted vegetables, or bread.

items.fix: Pat the bird dry before rubbing with marinade; cook at full heat, 220°C, without covering
items.cause: Bird not dried before marinating, or oven not hot enough
items.symptom: Skin is pale and soft rather than golden and crackled

items.fix: Make sure the bird is pressed completely flat so everything is at the same level; position legs slightly closer to the heat source
items.cause: Bird not spatchcocked flat, or legs shielded from heat
items.symptom: Breast is dry before the legs are done

items.fix: Use 1.5 tsp chilli flakes minimum; marinate for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight
items.cause: Not enough chilli, or marinated for too short a time

items.fix: Pat off excess marinade before roasting; concentrate the heat in the last 5 minutes if more colour is needed
items.cause: Sugar from the lemon juice caramelising too fast at high heat

content.text: Pollo alla diavola is a staple of the Roman trattoria. It has been on fixed-price menus there for at least a century. The 19th-century cookery writer Pellegrino Artusi described it in 1891 as a bird pressed under a weight over coals, the original grill. He noted that the name comes from the red colour of the chilli and the fierce heat of the cooking. The technique is probably older than his recording of it. The dish needs almost nothing beyond a good bird and time to marinate. It is one of the most satisfying returns in Italian cooking for the effort involved.
